maintainability.title: Maintainability
maintainability.description: Evaluates maintainability signals across modules and functions, with emphasis on complexity and long-term edit risk.
what_it_finds: Repeated logic and deep branching patterns

severity_focus.description: Indicates poor maintainability and high technical debt. Typically caused by extremely low MI (≤ 10) or high cyclomatic complexity (>20). Code at this level is difficult to extend, debug, or reuse.

Moderate maintainability issues that require refactoring or cleanup. Caused by MI between 10-20, moderate complexity (>10), or duplication. Such code is maintainable but may lead to higher future maintenance cost.
severity_focus.example: Maintainability Index 10-20, Cyclomatic Complexity 10-20, Function/Code Duplication

severity_focus.description: Codebase is generally maintainable with low complexity and high MI. No immediate action required but monitor quality regularly.
severity_focus.example: Maintainability Index > 20, System Rank: Highly/Fairly Maintainable

extra_sections.title: Terms
rows: Cyclomatic Complexity measures the number of independent execution paths in a function. It increases with branching, loops, and conditional logic.
Maintainability rank is derived from aggregated function complexity distribution. Rank lowers as low/high/very_high complexity percentages exceed thresholds for the analyzed codebase.

readability.title: Readability
readability.description: Flags Python code style and readability issues to improve consistency and comprehension.
what_it_finds: Missing docstrings and weak documentation patterns
Overly long lines and complex function bodies
Unused imports/variables and minor style violations

severity_focus.description: Significant readability or documentation gaps that increase onboarding and maintenance cost.
severity_focus.example: Critical docstring/clarity issues, repeated style violations

severity_focus.description: Moderate quality concerns in style and structure; worth cleaning in next hardening pass.
severity_focus.example: Mixed style patterns, repeated long lines, heavy nesting

severity_focus.description: Minor cleanup opportunities that improve consistency over time.

robustness.title: Robustness
robustness.description: Detects coding practices that reduce runtime safety and increase failure risk.

severity_focus.description: Issues that can directly cause runtime failure or exploitable behavior.
severity_focus.example: Dangerous security patterns or unsafe operations like Return value mismatch, invalid arguement type, invalid return statement

severity_focus.description: Potential stability concerns that degrade reliability without immediate failure.
severity_focus.example: Potentially unsafe patterns with limited risk like Wrong Operator, missing/invalid type argument

severity_focus.description: Minor robustness improvements and low-impact suggestions.
severity_focus.example: Style-level robustness improvements like missing function annotations, call to untyped function

observability.title: Observability
observability.description: Checks runtime visibility so incidents can be diagnosed and traced with less ambiguity.
what_it_finds: Low coverage of observability across files

severity_focus.description: Critical observability gaps that can hide production failures or block debugging. These include missing logs in critical functions or overall observability score below threshold.
severity_focus.example: Overall observability score < 30, critical function without logging

severity_focus.description: Files or modules with insufficient logging or limited instrumentation, reducing incident traceability.
Logging is present but not structured or standardized, making correlation and deep analysis harder.
Files with fair observability that meet minimum standards but can still benefit from richer logs.

extra_sections.title: What is a Critical Function?
rows: Any function that includes patterns like error/exception handling, authentication/authorization, processing, validation, or functions with try/except/raise usage. These functions are expected to emit logs because failures here have higher observability impact.
